description: Three structural reforms targeting the governance void that produces exclusive inclusion. The reforms are mutually reinforcing; each addresses one architectural feature of the void. All three are required for a complete solution.
mutual_reinforcement: Standing without enforceable standards is empty. Standards without standing are unenforceable. External accountability without internal mechanisms can be satisfied on paper. Together, the three reforms create overlapping accountability redundancy that addresses each feature of the governance void.

reforms.one_sentence: Legislatures permit currently enrolled students and currently employed faculty to bring derivative actions enforcing existing fiduciary duties against nonprofit educational boards.

legal_model.primary: Corporate derivative suit (Fed. R. Civ. P. 23.1 analog)
legal_model.description: Derivative suits allow shareholders in for-profit corporations to enforce fiduciary duties on behalf of the corporation when the board refuses to act. The same mechanism can be legislatively extended to educational stakeholders, with modifications to address the nonprofit context.
legal_model.corporate_analogy: Decades of corporate derivative suit experience establish the feasibility of the mechanism; courts have developed sophisticated doctrines for managing strike suits, board deference, and fee-shifting that can be adapted to the nonprofit educational context.

case_precedent.holding: Director of nonprofit public benefit corporation brought derivative action alleging breach of fiduciary duties by co-directors of the Conrad Prebys Foundation. The Court of Appeal held: (1) director lost standing to continue derivative action once her term as director expired and she was not reelected; (2) director similarly lost standing under charitable trust, self-dealing, and removal statutes; but (3) remand required for Superior Court to determine whether Attorney General or some other relator should step into plaintiff's role to continue litigation.
case_precedent.relevance_to_reform: Turner v. Victoria establishes three things directly relevant to stakeholder standing reform: (a) derivative-style litigation against nonprofit boards is available in principle; (b) current law makes standing precarious because it is tied to governance position rather than affected-party status; (c) the court's direction to consider relator substitution suggests judicial receptivity to standing being held by parties other than board members. Legislative reform creating enrolled-student and current-faculty standing addresses the standing-precarity problem Turner identified.

mechanism.who_can_sue: Currently enrolled students (at time of filing) and currently employed faculty (at time of filing) of the institution
mechanism.on_behalf_of: The institution itself (derivative action), enforcing existing fiduciary duties against the board
mechanism.what_can_be_challenged: Board decisions or patterns of decisions that breach the duty of care, loyalty, or obedience in ways that produce systematic identity-based exclusion or fail to enforce the institution's stated mission
mechanism.threshold_showing: Plaintiff must allege specific facts demonstrating: (1) the institution owed a fiduciary duty relevant to the challenged conduct; (2) the board breached that duty; (3) the breach produced or is likely to produce harm to the plaintiff or the identity group of which plaintiff is a member

procedural_safeguards.rationale: Safeguards are designed to deter strike suits and excessive litigation while preserving the deterrent effect and accountability function of derivative standing

safeguards.name: 90-Day Demand Requirement
safeguards.description: Before filing suit, plaintiff must make written demand on the board to take action to remedy the alleged breach; plaintiff may file suit only if the board refuses, fails to respond within 90 days, or responds in bad faith
safeguards.purpose: Gives the board opportunity to self-correct without litigation; filters out cases where internal governance can still function; builds record for any subsequent litigation
safeguards.exception: Demand may be excused where plaintiff can demonstrate demand would be futile — e.g., all board members are conflicted in the challenged decision

safeguards.name: Heightened Pleading Standards
safeguards.description: Modeled on Fed. R. Civ. P. 23.1 requiring plaintiff to allege with particularity the efforts made to obtain the desired action and the reasons for the plaintiff's failure to obtain the action; applied to the educational context, plaintiff must allege specific facts (not conclusory allegations) about the fiduciary breach and identity-based exclusion
safeguards.purpose: Filters out conclusory complaints and policy disagreements dressed as fiduciary breach claims; ensures courts review only cognizable governance failures, not educational policy disputes

safeguards.name: Court-Set Bond Requirement with Hardship Waiver
safeguards.description: Court may require plaintiff to post bond to cover defendant's reasonable litigation costs if plaintiff does not prevail; court has discretion to waive or reduce bond on demonstrated financial hardship
safeguards.purpose: Deters frivolous claims; hardship waiver preserves access for legitimate claims by resource-constrained students

safeguards.name: Fee-Shifting for Prevailing Plaintiffs; Sanctions for Bad-Faith Claims
safeguards.description: Prevailing plaintiffs who substantially enforce a fiduciary duty are entitled to attorney's fees from the institution (consistent with common fund doctrine in corporate derivative suits); plaintiffs who bring frivolous or bad-faith claims are subject to fee-shifting against them
safeguards.purpose: Incentivizes legitimate claims; compensates plaintiffs who provide a governance benefit; deters strategic litigation

safeguards.name: Abuse-of-Discretion Judicial Review
safeguards.description: Courts review board decisions under an abuse-of-discretion standard (not de novo), preserving board deference for good-faith business judgments while enabling accountability for arbitrary or bad-faith decisions
safeguards.purpose: Preserves appropriate board discretion and institutional governance autonomy; prevents courts from substituting their educational policy judgments for boards'; still enables review where the governance void is operating

reforms.primary_effect: Deterrent: boards exercise greater care knowing stakeholders can enforce duties. Even if few suits are ever filed, the credible threat of litigation changes ex ante decision-making at the board level.
reforms.secondary_effect: Information: the demand process generates information about board conduct that may be useful to state Attorneys General, accreditation bodies, and the public even when no suit is filed.
reforms.assessment: Most legally grounded of the three reforms. Decades of corporate derivative suit experience provide administrative template. Deterrent effect may be sufficient alone to change board behavior. Most feasible for state legislative action without federal constitutional complications.

reforms.one_sentence: State legislatures require tax-exempt educational institutions to include specific, measurable commitments to equal treatment in mission statements as a condition of charitable registration, converting the hollow duty of obedience into a justiciable standard.

legal_model.description: The Robertson litigation established that when a mission is specific enough, courts can evaluate whether board conduct is consistent with that mission. Mission Specificity reform extends this principle by requiring institutions to adopt specific missions as a condition of operating as a tax-exempt charity.

case_precedent.citation: No. C-99-02 (N.J. Super. Ct., Mercer Cty., 2007 WL 7687562 (Oct. 25, 2007))
case_precedent.background: Marie Robertson, heir to the Great Atlantic & Pacific Tea Company, donated approximately 700,000 shares of A&P stock to Princeton in 1961 — ultimately worth hundreds of millions of dollars. The Robertson Foundation was incorporated with a specific mission: 'to strengthen the Government of the United States and increase its ability and determination to defend and extend freedom throughout the world by improving the facilities for the training and education of men and women for government service.' Princeton was alleged to have diverted foundation assets to general university purposes inconsistent with this specific mission.
case_precedent.holding: Court denied summary judgment on fiduciary duties and business judgment rule, ruling that fiduciary duties and the business judgment rule both applied to foundation trustees — but the denial of summary judgment meant the case could proceed to determine whether Princeton's conduct breached those duties. The specificity of the mission statement (government service preparation) gave the court a judicially manageable standard against which to measure conduct.
case_precedent.settlement: Before final judgment, parties settled. Robertson family received approximately $50 million paid to a new independent Robertson Foundation; total recovery including legal fees was approximately $90 million — demonstrating that courts can effectively evaluate institutional conduct when missions are specific.
case_precedent.relevance_to_reform: Robertson demonstrates: (a) mission specificity is the key that unlocks judicial review; (b) when donors specify a purpose with enough precision, courts can evaluate whether institutions honored it; (c) the same principle can be generalized through legislation requiring all tax-exempt educational institutions to adopt operationally specific equal-treatment commitments.

mechanism.legislative_requirement: State legislatures, as a condition of charitable registration and continued tax-exempt status, require educational institutions to adopt mission statements that include specific, measurable commitments to equal treatment — not aspirational language but operationally testable standards
mechanism.specificity_standard: A mission commitment is sufficiently specific if it generates a judicially manageable standard: a reviewing court must be able to determine, from the mission text alone and objective facts, whether the institution has honored its commitment in a given case
examples_of_insufficient_language: 'We are committed to an inclusive community' — not judicially manageable; no operational benchmark
'We strive toward a more just world' — aspirational; no behavioral standard
'Diversity, equity, and inclusion are core values' — values statement, not enforceable commitment
examples_of_sufficient_language: 'The institution will investigate all reported identity-based exclusion within 30 days, using procedures materially equal to those used for other civil rights complaints, and will provide written findings to complainants'

'The institution's governance processes will not systematically use identity-based criteria in hiring, promotion, or speaker-selection decisions; the institution will maintain records sufficient to permit review of this commitment'
'The institution will allocate civil rights enforcement resources equitably across identity categories recognized by federal law'

reforms.how_it_works: Converts hollow duty of obedience into justiciable standard. Once mission includes specific equal-treatment commitment, affected stakeholders (with derivative standing under Reform 1) can invoke the duty of obedience to challenge departures from that commitment. Board cannot invoke business judgment rule deference for decisions that facially contradict the institution's stated and legally required specific mission.

palmiter.citation: Alan R. Palmiter, 'Duty of Obedience: The Forgotten Duty,' 55 N.Y.L. Sch. L. Rev. 457 (2011)
palmiter.holding: The duty of obedience requires fiduciaries to abide by legal norms both internal and external to the corporation. In for-profit corporations this duty has been subsumed into 'good faith'; in nonprofit corporations it remains distinct. The duty compels fiduciaries to honor the organization's constitutive documents, donor conditions, and applicable law. When mission statements are vague, the duty is formal rather than operative.
palmiter.reform_application: Palmiter's framework shows that the duty of obedience exists and has content; the problem is that vague missions empty it of operational force. Mission Specificity reform fills the duty with content by requiring specific commitments — restoring the duty's operative force.

buckles.citation: Johnny Rex Buckles, 'How Deep Are the Springs of Obedience Norms That Bind the Overseers of Charities?' 62 Cath. U. L. Rev. 913 (2013)

taxonomy.legality_norm: Fiduciaries must ensure the organization complies with applicable law
taxonomy.dynamic_charter_fidelity_norm: Fiduciaries must honor the organization's constitutive documents as amended
taxonomy.static_charter_fidelity_norm: Fiduciaries must honor the original constitutive documents even when the organization has formally amended them, if assets were donated under the original terms
taxonomy.dynamic_mission_fidelity_norm: Fiduciaries must honor the organization's current mission as a whole, including interpretive evolution
taxonomy.historic_mission_fidelity_norm: Fiduciaries must honor the organization's historic mission purpose even when current documents have evolved away from it
taxonomy.charity_advancement_norm: Fiduciaries must actively advance the organization's charitable purposes, not merely avoid violating them

buckles.reform_application: Buckles's taxonomy provides the framework for interpreting a specific mission commitment: once an institution has adopted a specific equal-treatment commitment as part of its mission, the Dynamic Charter Fidelity Norm, Dynamic Mission Fidelity Norm, and Charity Advancement Norm all require fiduciaries to honor and advance that commitment — not merely avoid facially violating it. The taxonomy transforms a legislatively required mission commitment into a multi-layered fiduciary obligation.

reforms.assessment: Requires doctrinal development: state legislatures must mandate specificity as a condition of charitable registration, and courts must be willing to review mission compliance on the merits. Robertson precedent shows the principle is sound. Needs legislative action mandating the specificity requirement — institutions will not voluntarily adopt specific missions that constrain their discretion. More feasible at state level than federal level given First Amendment concerns about federal government dictating institutional speech.

reforms.one_sentence: IRS conditions 501(c)(3) tax exemption on demonstrated governance structures preventing systematic identity-based exclusion, modeled on the hospital community benefit standard.

legal_model.primary: Hospital community benefit standard (Rev. Rul. 69-545; I.R.C. § 501(r))
legal_model.description: Hospitals must demonstrate community benefit to maintain 501(c)(3) status; this requirement has forced structural changes in hospital governance, reporting, and accountability. The same mechanism can be applied to universities, conditioning exemption on demonstrated governance structures rather than aspirational policy statements.

hospital_analogy.revenue_ruling: Rev. Rul. 69-545 (1969): IRS established that nonprofit hospitals must benefit the community to qualify for tax exemption; this was operationalized through charity care, emergency treatment, and community health needs assessment requirements
hospital_analogy.section_501r: I.R.C. § 501(r) (enacted 2010): Required nonprofit hospitals to conduct community health needs assessments every three years, adopt implementation strategies, and make results publicly available; failure to comply results in loss of tax-exempt status and a 10% excise tax. This demonstrates that operational test requirements with specific governance process obligations are legally and administratively feasible for 501(c)(3) institutions.

university_context.current_exemption_value: Universities receive approximately $37 billion annually in federal tax exemptions plus additional federal research funding, but face no comparable accountability requirement to hospitals regarding whether their governance structures prevent systematic exclusion of stakeholders
university_context.proposed_test: IRS would condition 501(c)(3) exemption on demonstrated governance structures: (a) board composition or selection process providing meaningful accountability to affected stakeholders; (b) mission statement meeting specificity standard (linking to Reform 2); (c) documented internal grievance process for identity-based exclusion claims with defined timelines; (d) periodic reporting to IRS on governance structure and identity-equity outcomes

constitutional_analysis.potential_objection: First Amendment unconstitutional conditions: conditioning tax exemption on institutional governance structure might be challenged as compelling institutional speech or association

response.primary: Rumsfeld v. FAIR, 547 U.S. 47 (2006): The Supreme Court held 8-0 that conditioning federal funding on equal access for military recruiters did not violate universities' First Amendment rights because the condition regulated conduct — the provision of equal access — not speech. 'The Solomon Amendment neither limits what law schools may say nor requires them to say anything.' Any speech burden incidental to the access requirement was 'plainly incidental to the Solomon Amendment's regulation of conduct.'
response.application: The operational test targets governance structures — board composition, internal grievance processes, reporting requirements — which are conduct, not speech. Like the Solomon Amendment, the test does not dictate what institutions may say about identity, inclusion, or any other topic. It requires only that governance structures provide accountability for the institutional conduct (identity-based exclusion) that the test addresses. Under FAIR, this is constitutional.
response.additional_basis: South Dakota v. Dole, 483 U.S. 203 (1987): Congress may condition federal benefits on the recipient meeting specified requirements if the conditions are related to the federal interest in the project. Tax exemption for public-benefit organizations has as its rationale that such organizations benefit the public; requiring governance structures that prevent systematic exclusion of public stakeholders is directly related to that rationale.

constitutional_analysis.first_amendment_limit: The operational test must not condition exemption on the content of institutional speech, political positions, or viewpoint. Conditioning on governance process (how institutions make and review decisions) is constitutionally distinct from conditioning on speech content (what institutions say or believe). Reform 3 is drafted to target governance process exclusively.

what_is_required: Board accountability mechanism: institution must demonstrate one or more of: (a) external stakeholder representation on the board with voting rights; (b) regular elections of board members by a defined stakeholder constituency; (c) independent board audit by a qualified external party with public reporting

Internal grievance infrastructure: documented, publicly available process for identity-based exclusion complaints; defined response timeline (suggested: 30 days for acknowledgment, 90 days for resolution); written findings provided to complainants; annual aggregate reporting to IRS
Mission specificity compliance: mission statement meets the specificity standard established under Reform 2 (or equivalent state law)
Periodic IRS reporting: triennial report documenting governance structure compliance, grievance process outcomes, and identity-equity measures, modeled on the 501(r) community health needs assessment
mechanism.enforcement: IRS revocation of 501(c)(3) status for non-compliance; transition period of three years from enactment for institutions to come into compliance; excise tax on investment income (consistent with I.R.C. § 4968 for large university endowments) for institutions that do not meet governance standards

reforms.assessment: Most powerful of the three reforms: conditioning tax exemption creates federal leverage simultaneously over all 501(c)(3) educational institutions, unlike state-by-state litigation or legislation. Most legally robust given FAIR precedent and South Dakota v. Dole. Most politically fragile: administratively reversible by executive branch through IRS guidance; most dependent on continued political support. Most likely to generate constitutional challenge despite the FAIR precedent. Best deployed in combination with Reforms 1 and 2, which provide state-level and judicial redundancy if the federal test is reversed or weakened.

integration.why_all_three: Each reform targets one architectural feature of the governance void. Standing (Reform 1) addresses the absence of stakeholder enforcement. Mission Specificity (Reform 2) addresses the hollow duty of obedience. Operational Test (Reform 3) addresses the self-perpetuating board's insulation from external accountability. All three are necessary because each can be circumvented without the others: standing without enforceable standards produces empty lawsuits; specificity without standing produces unenforceable promises; external accountability without internal mechanisms produces paper compliance.
integration.if_only_one_reform: If political conditions allow only one reform, stakeholder standing (Reform 1) is the most legally grounded, most feasible at the state level, and most likely to produce lasting structural change. Its deterrent effect on board conduct may be sufficient to incentivize both mission specificity and operational accountability even without the other reforms being legislatively required.
integration.sequence: Reform 1 (standing) can be enacted by any state through its nonprofit corporation law. Reform 2 (mission specificity) is most effective if enacted simultaneously with Reform 1, giving standing-enabled stakeholders an enforceable standard. Reform 3 (operational test) is most effective as a federal complement to state-level Reforms 1 and 2, creating overlapping accountability that does not depend on any single political environment.
